The way we write code is changing

Here’s what the landscape looks like right now.

Terminal agents

Lightweight, portable, and easy to run multiple sessions at once. No IDE overhead in the context window.

Examples: Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot CLI

  • Claude Code popularized the terminal agent category. Has the most built-in features of any CLI agent. Not open source, but the source was accidentally published in March 2026. No data retention for training.
  • Codex CLI is open source (Rust). Telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default.
  • Gemini CLI is open source. Telemetry configurable via .gemini/settings.json.
  • GitHub Copilot CLI is closed source. Telemetry opt-in. Supports COPILOT_OFFLINE=true to disable all telemetry.

IDEs

Completions, chat, agent modes. Your code stays front and center. This is mostly about preference.

Examples: VS Code + Copilot, Cursor, Antigravity, Windsurf

  • VS Code + Copilot is the biggest ecosystem. VS Code is open source, Copilot is not. Telemetry enabled by default but configurable. Enterprise tier offers no-code-retention.
  • Cursor is closed source. Cursor 3 (April 2026) added an Agents Window for running parallel agents across repos. Privacy Mode disables telemetry and code retention.
  • Antigravity is closed source (Google, Gemini-powered). Has both an Editor view and a Manager view for orchestrating multiple agents in parallel.
  • Windsurf is a VS Code fork (open source base, closed extensions). Cascade streams agent changes in real time with automatic context retrieval.

Agent-first workflows

Every major tool is building a dedicated surface for this.

  • Claude desktop app visual diffs, drag-and-drop pane layout, parallel sessions with git isolation. Good entry point if you don’t live in the terminal.
  • Codex App is a standalone command center. Parallel agents in isolated worktrees. Free tier available.
  • Cursor Agents Window (Cursor 3, April 2026) is a full-screen tiled workspace for running agents across repos, locally or in the cloud.
  • VS Code Agents app is a companion app for running agents across multiple repos in parallel. Expected May 2026.

The same agent, everywhere

The biggest shift in 2026: the tool you use matters less than the agent behind it. The same agent now shows up across terminal, IDE, desktop, web, and phone. Pick the one you trust, then use it from whatever surface fits the moment.

TerminalIDE (extension)DesktopWebPhone
AnthropicClaude CodeextensionCoworkclaude.ai/codeClaude app
OpenAICodex CLIextensionCodex Appchatgpt.com/codexChatGPT app
GitHubCopilot CLIbuilt-in—github.com/copilotGitHub Mobile
GoogleGemini CLIAntigravity—gemini.google.com—

Voice typing
You’re typing more natural language than ever. Speaking is 3x faster. Built-in dictation is free (macOS F5 🎤, Windows Win+H). For more, see SuperWhisper (on-device), Wispr Flow (cloud), or Talon (full hands-free control).

The lines are blurring. Most tools now span multiple categories. This page only covers a few - there are many more worth exploring.

Exercise

  1. Pick the AI tool you use most. Check what other interfaces it offers - web, mobile, desktop app, IDE extension, CLI. Try one you haven’t used before.
  2. Explore a tool you’ve never used. Open a project and see how it feels.

Three ways to work with AI

Every AI coding tool falls somewhere across three categories. They’re not rigid boxes. Most tools are blurring the lines, and most developers use more than one. But understanding the categories helps you pick a starting point.

CLI - The terminal is your interface. You type alongside the agent, it reads files, runs commands, edits code. You see everything it does. Best for developers who live in the terminal and want full control.

Examples: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Aider

IDE - Your code editor is the interface. AI lives in a sidebar, suggests completions, answers questions in chat, and can make edits across files. Your code is always front and center.

Examples: VS Code + Copilot, JetBrains + AI Assistant

Agent-first app - The agent dashboard is the interface. You give tasks, the agent works, you review results. Code editing is secondary to task management. Think of it as going from writing code to managing agents who write code.

Examples: Codex desktop, Cursor 3’s Agents Window

It’s not one or the other

Most productive developers in 2026 use 2-3 tools. A common setup:

  • IDE for daily editing and quick completions
  • CLI for complex multi-file tasks and hard problems
  • Agent-first for parallel tasks you want to run in the background

The tools are also converging. Cursor is an IDE that added an agent-first window. Claude Code is a CLI that has Cowork for background tasks. Copilot added agent mode. The categories describe where the tool started, not where it’s stuck.

Exercise

  1. Ask your tool: “What kind of tool are you? CLI, IDE, or agent-first?”
  2. Think about your last coding session. Were you mostly editing code, running terminal commands, or delegating tasks? That tells you which category fits your current workflow.

Start with how you work

Don’t pick a tool based on hype. Pick based on what’s in front of you when you’re working.

What’s in front of youCategoryExamples
A terminalCLIClaude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Aider
A code editorIDEVS Code + Copilot, JetBrains + AI Assistant
An agent dashboardAgent-first appCodex desktop, Cursor 3

CLI tools are interactive. You and the agent work together in real time. You see every file it reads, every command it runs. Great for hard problems where you want to stay close to the action.

IDE tools meet you where you already are. Completions, inline chat, background suggestions. You keep editing, AI keeps helping. Lowest friction if you already have an editor you love.

Agent-first apps flip the model. You describe what you want, spin up agents, and review what comes back. Less hands-on, more delegation. Best when you have clear tasks you can hand off.

The lines are blurring

These aren’t rigid boxes. Cursor started as an IDE and just shipped an agent-first window. Claude Code is a CLI but also has Cowork for background tasks. Copilot added agent mode. Every tool is racing toward “all three.”

Most developers in 2026 use 2-3 tools. 90% use at least one AI tool at work. The question isn’t “which one” but “which combination.”

Exercise

  1. What tool are you using right now? Ask it: “Are you a CLI, an IDE extension, or an agent-first app?”
  2. Try a tool from a different category than your usual. If you’re an IDE person, try a CLI agent for one task this week.

The three interfaces

The AI model behind your tool matters less than the interface you use to work with it. The same model can feel completely different depending on whether you’re in a terminal, an editor, or an agent dashboard.

CLI

You’re in a terminal. You and the agent have a conversation. It reads your files, runs your tests, edits your code, all visible. You’re pair programming.

Good for: Complex multi-file tasks. Debugging. Working across repos. Developers who think in commands.

Tools: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Aider

IDE

You’re in your editor. AI suggests completions, answers questions in a sidebar, and can edit files in the background. Your code stays front and center.

Good for: Daily editing. Quick questions. Inline suggestions. Developers who don’t want to change their workflow.

Tools: VS Code + Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf, Antigravity

Agent-first app

You’re looking at an agent dashboard. You describe tasks, spin up agents (sometimes many at once), and review results when they’re done. Code is secondary to task management.

Good for: Parallel tasks. Delegation. Reviewing rather than writing. Developers managing agents rather than writing every line.

Tools: Codex desktop app, Cursor 3’s Agents Window

You’ll probably use more than one

90% of developers use at least one AI tool at work. Most productive ones use 2-3. A common stack:

  • IDE for everyday editing (Copilot for completions)
  • CLI for the hard stuff (Claude Code for complex refactors)
  • Agent-first for background work (spin up tasks and review later)

The tools are converging. Cursor has both an IDE and an agent window. Claude Code has Cowork for background tasks. Copilot added agent mode. Don’t pick one forever. Pick one to start, then expand.

Exercise

  1. Ask your current tool: “What type of tool are you? Describe your interface model.”
  2. Think about a recent frustrating coding task. Would a different category of tool have been a better fit?

The landscape is shifting

A year ago, “AI coding tool” meant autocomplete in your editor. Now there are terminals that edit your code, IDEs that run agents, desktop apps that spin up parallel tasks, browser tools that build full apps from a prompt, and autonomous agents that work while you sleep.

This isn’t about picking the best one. It’s about knowing what exists so you can match the right interface to the work in front of you.

Terminal agents

You type a prompt. The agent reads your files, edits code, runs commands, and reports back. Everything happens in your terminal. You see what it sees.

Tools: Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot CLI

AI-integrated IDEs

Your editor, but with AI woven in. Completions as you type, a chat sidebar for questions, and agent modes that can plan and execute multi-file changes in the background. Your code stays front and center.

Tools: VS Code + Copilot, Cursor, Antigravity, Windsurf

Browser-based builders

You describe what you want in a browser. The tool generates a working app, sometimes with hosting, a database, and auth included. No local setup required. Good for prototypes, MVPs, and people who don’t want to touch a terminal.

Tools: Replit, bolt.new, v0, Lovable

Autonomous agents

You file an issue or describe a task. An agent picks it up, plans an approach, writes code, runs tests, and opens a PR. You review the output, not the process. Some run in the cloud, some run locally in the background.

Tools: Devin, GitHub Copilot coding agent, Claude Code (background agents)

The lines are blurring

Most tools don’t fit neatly into one box anymore:

  • Cursor has an IDE and an agent window
  • Claude Code runs interactively or as a background agent
  • GitHub Copilot works in the CLI, the IDE, and the browser
  • Windsurf was acquired by Cognition (who makes Devin)

You don’t need to pick one category. Most developers use 2-3 tools. The question isn’t “which is best” but “which interface fits this task right now.”

Exercise

Open two different AI coding tools and give them the same task. Notice how the interface changes the way you work, not just the output.

How we work is changing

There used to be one way to write code: you, an editor, a terminal. Now there are at least five different interfaces for working with AI on code. Each one changes how you think, what you delegate, and what you keep control of.

This kata isn’t a recommendation. It’s a map.

The terminal

A conversation in your terminal. You talk, the agent acts. It reads files, runs tests, edits code. You watch it work. Transparent and direct.

Tools: Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot CLI

The editor

Your IDE, with AI layered in. Inline completions, a chat panel, agent modes that work across files. You stay in the flow of editing. The AI meets you where you already work.

Tools: VS Code + Copilot, Cursor, Antigravity, Windsurf

The browser

No install, no clone, no local environment. You open a URL, describe what you want, and a working app appears. Some include hosting, databases, and deployment. The target audience includes developers prototyping fast and non-developers building for the first time.

Tools: Replit, bolt.new, v0, Lovable

The agent dashboard

You manage tasks, not code. Spin up agents, assign work, review results. Some tools let you run multiple agents in parallel. You’re a reviewer, not a typist.

Tools: Devin, Cursor Agents Window, Codex desktop app

The background

You don’t interact at all. An agent picks up a GitHub issue, writes code, runs tests, and opens a PR. You review asynchronously, like reviewing a teammate’s work.

Tools: GitHub Copilot coding agent, Claude Code background agents, Devin

What this means for you

You don’t need to pick one. Most developers are already using 2-3 tools across these categories. The right question isn’t “which tool” but “which mode of working fits this moment”:

  • Writing new code in a familiar codebase? The editor.
  • Debugging a hairy cross-file problem? The terminal.
  • Prototyping an idea from scratch? The browser.
  • Delegating grunt work? The background.
  • Managing a batch of tasks? The agent dashboard.

The tools are converging fast. Cursor has an editor and a dashboard. Claude Code runs interactively and in the background. Copilot lives in the CLI, the editor, and the browser. Awareness of the landscape matters more than loyalty to a single tool.

Exercise

  1. Count how many AI coding interfaces you’ve used in the last month. Terminal? Editor? Browser? Background?
  2. Think about a task you did recently. Was the interface you used the best fit, or just the one you had open?

The model matters less than the interface

Claude, GPT, Gemini — they all write decent code. What actually changes your experience is the interface sitting between you and the model. The same developer, same codebase, same problem will work completely differently in a terminal versus a browser versus a background agent.

Right now there are five distinct ways to work with AI on code. Most developers don’t know all five exist. This is about awareness, not ranking.

1. Terminal agents

A conversation in your shell. You prompt, the agent reads your repo, edits files, runs commands. Everything is visible. You’re pair programming with someone who types faster than you.

Tools: Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot CLI

2. AI-native editors

Your editor with AI built in. Completions as you type, chat in a sidebar, agent modes for bigger changes. Your code is front and center. The AI assists without taking over.

Tools: VS Code + Copilot, Cursor, Antigravity, Windsurf

3. Browser builders

Open a URL. Describe what you want. Get a working app. No git clone, no node_modules, no local setup. Some include hosting and a database out of the box. The fastest path from idea to something running.

Tools: Replit, bolt.new, v0, Lovable

4. Agent dashboards

You manage work, not code. Describe tasks, spin up agents (sometimes several at once), review what comes back. The interface looks more like a project board than an editor.

Tools: Devin, Cursor Agents Window, Codex desktop app

5. Background agents

You don’t interact in real time. An agent picks up a GitHub issue, plans an approach, writes code, runs tests, opens a PR. You review the result like you’d review a coworker’s pull request.

Tools: GitHub Copilot coding agent, Claude Code background agents, Devin

The boundaries are dissolving

These categories are already merging:

ToolTerminalEditorDashboardBackground
Claude CodeYesVS Code extension—Yes (Cowork)
Cursor—YesYes (Agents Window)—
GitHub CopilotYesYes—Yes (coding agent)
Devin——YesYes

A year from now this table will look different. The point isn’t to memorize it. The point is to notice that you have options and that different work calls for different interfaces.

Exercise

  1. Look at the five categories above. Which ones have you tried? Which ones haven’t you touched?
  2. Pick a category you haven’t used and try one tool from it this week. Even five minutes changes your mental model.

Your session isn’t blank

Every AI coding tool loads context before you say anything. Before you type a single word, you’re already using 20-30k tokens. This usually includes:

  • Project instructions from a file in your repo (CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .cursor/rules/, GEMINI.md). This is how you tell the tool about your stack, conventions, and preferences.
  • Connected tools like MCP servers that give the AI access to external services.
  • Memory from past sessions, things the tool learned about you or your project over time.
  • User-level config stored outside your repo (like ~/.claude/, ~/.gemini/, or VS Code’s user settings). These follow you across every project.

Most tools have a way to inspect what’s loaded. Find yours.

In Claude Code, type /context to see everything in the current session.

In VS Code Copilot, hover the token circle at the bottom right of the chat input.

Exercise

  1. Open your tool and find where it shows loaded context. Not sure how? Ask the AI itself, check your tool’s settings, or look at the docs.
  2. Try these prompts and see what comes back:
    • “Without looking at any code, what do you know about this project?”
    • “Without doing any research, what are my preferences?”

Magic box not empty

You open tool. You say nothing. But magic box ALREADY KNOW THINGS. It eat 20-30 thousand tiny word-pieces before you even grunt.

How? Magic box eat these things:

  • Cave rules file in your code cave (CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md). This tell magic box “how we do things in THIS cave.”
  • Connected magic pipes (MCP). Other tools magic box can reach. Maybe it grab things from GitHub cave or Slack cave. You not know unless you look.
  • Old memories. Magic box remember things from LAST TIME. What you like, what make you angry, what you told it about your code cave.
  • Your personal cave markings stored outside code cave (like ~/.claude/). Follow you everywhere, every cave.

Most tool have way to see what already inside. Find yours.

In Claude Code, type /context. See everything magic box already eating.

In VS Code Copilot, hover little circle at bottom right. It show how full magic box belly is.

Exercise

  1. Open tool. Find where it show what already inside. Not know how? Grunt at it: “How me see what you already know?”
  2. Try these:
    • “Without peeking at code, what you know about this place?”
    • “Without snooping around, what you know about ME?”
TermWhat you need to know
TokenThe unit AI reads and writes in. ~3/4 of a word. Everything costs tokens. A 200-line file is about 800.
Context windowThe AI’s memory limit for one session. Everything has to fit: your messages, its responses, files, instructions. When full, older content gets compacted or summarized.
ModelThe specific AI brain (Opus, Sonnet, GPT-4). Different speed, cost, and smarts. You can usually switch.
PromptEverything sent to the model. Not just your message, but system instructions, files, and history bundled together.
System promptHidden instructions your tool sends before you type anything. Your CLAUDE.md or copilot-instructions.md lives here.
SkillsSpecialized knowledge your agent loads on demand, not upfront. Like downloading kung fu in The Matrix. You can invoke them yourself (/review-pr) or the agent can auto-load them when it recognizes key phrases in what you’re asking.
AgentAI that takes actions: reads files, runs tests, edits code, creates commits. Most AI coding tools are agents.
SubagentA child agent that works in its own context, separate from the main conversation. Often scoped to a single task or skill (e.g. “research this”, “review that file”). Keeps your main thread clean - the subagent does the work and comes back with just the important bits.
MCPModel Context Protocol. Plug-in system connecting your AI to external services. e.g. an MCP server that lets your AI read and create GitHub issues.
HooksAutomated actions that fire at specific points in the agent’s workflow. Unlike skills, hooks run outside the AI’s reasoning. They enforce rules the AI can’t skip. e.g. “always run lint before committing.”
Commands / Slash commandsShortcuts built into your tool (like /context, /init, /memory). Increasingly these are just skills you can invoke by name.
Rules / InstructionsConfiguration files that tell the AI how to behave in your project. e.g. “always use yarn, never npm” in your CLAUDE.md. Loaded every session automatically.
Plugins / Extensions / CustomizationsInstallable packages that bundle skills, MCP servers, hooks, and commands together. One plugin might add Slack integration, a /deploy command, and a pre-commit hook all at once.
Human-in-the-loopA workflow where the AI pauses for human approval before acting. Sometimes you want this (reviewing code before commit). Sometimes it’s a bottleneck (approving every file read). Tuning when to require it is a big part of agent setup.
Dark codeCode generated by AI that no human has read or understood. Builds up when you accept suggestions without reviewing. Creates invisible tech debt.
Agent orchestrationRunning multiple agents in parallel on different tasks. The Codex desktop app and Cursor 3 are built around this concept.
HarnessThe software that wraps the AI model and gives it tools, permissions, and rules. Claude Code, Cursor, and VS Code Copilot are all harnesses. The model is the brain, the harness is the body.
Spec-driven developmentWriting a structured spec before agents write code. Frameworks like BMAD, GitHub Spec Kit, and Kiro follow this pattern. The spec can be static (written once) or living (updates as code evolves).
WorktreeAn isolated git checkout so multiple agents can work on the same repo without stepping on each other. Most multi-agent tools create these automatically.
CompactionWhat happens when the context window fills up. The tool summarizes older messages to make room. You keep going, but lose detail from earlier in the conversation.
DispatchSending a task to an agent asynchronously. Assign an issue, close your laptop, come back to a PR.
Background agentAn agent running in a sandbox, not in your active session. You dispatch work to it and check back later. Cursor, Codex, and Copilot all have these.
TemperatureRandomness dial. Low = deterministic, predictable code. High = creative, varied output. Coding tools keep it low.
HallucinationAI confidently stating something false. e.g. referencing a function that doesn’t exist or inventing an API flag. Normal. Always verify.
RAGRetrieval-Augmented Generation. AI searches real documents before answering instead of guessing from training data.
Context engineeringDeliberately shaping what information the AI sees so it gives better results. The real skill in using AI tools well.
MemoryWhat your tool remembers between sessions. Your preferences, project context, past corrections.

Exercise

  1. Pick a term that sounds useful and ask your tool: “Why would I use [term]? Give me a real example.”
  2. Try: “ELI5 [term]” or “Explain [term] like a caveman.”
WordWhat it mean
TokenTiny word-piece. Magic box eat these. One word is like one berry. Everything cost berries.
Context windowHow much magic box can hold in head at once. When head full, it forget old things. Brain get smooshed.
ModelWhich magic box brain you talking to. Some brain fast but dumb. Some brain slow but very smart. You pick.
PromptEverything you shove into magic box mouth. Not just your grunt, but cave rules and old conversation too.
System promptSecret instructions cave leader put in magic box BEFORE you talk. You no see, but it change everything.
SkillsMagic box not born knowing everything. Skill is like downloading kung fu into brain RIGHT WHEN NEEDED. You can tell it (/review-pr) or magic box hear key words in your grunt and grab right scroll on its own.
AgentMagic box that DO things. Not just talk. It read your cave paintings, run your tests, change your code. Most tools now are agents.
SubagentScout you send out from cave. Does research in own head, not yours. Comes back with only the good stuff. Keeps your main cave brain clean and not full of junk.
MCPMagic pipe that connect magic box to other caves. GitHub cave, Slack cave, database cave. Like secret tunnel between caves.
HooksTrip wire. When magic box walk through certain point, trip wire ALWAYS go off. No thinking. No choice. e.g. “ALWAYS check fire before leave cave.”
Commands / Slash commandsSpecial grunts that start with /. Quick way to tell magic box do specific thing without big explanation.
Rules / InstructionsCave wall paintings that tell magic box how tribe does things. e.g. “We use yarn, NEVER npm.” Loaded every time.
Plugins / Extensions / CustomizationsGift basket you give magic box. Inside basket: skills, magic pipes, trip wires, special grunts. All bundled together. One basket might add Slack cave, deploy grunt, and safety trip wire.
Human-in-the-loopMagic box stop and wait for you to grunt “yes” before doing thing. Sometimes good (check before big change). Sometimes annoying (asking permission for every little thing). Finding right balance is key.
Dark codeCode magic box write that NO HUMAN LOOK AT. You just say “yes yes yes” without reading. Bad. Invisible danger pile up.
Agent orchestrationRunning MANY magic boxes at same time on different tasks. Like having whole tribe of magic boxes.
HarnessThe body around magic box brain. Brain think, harness do. Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code are harnesses. Brain without harness just sit there.
TemperatureHow wild magic box act. Low = same answer every time. High = creative, unpredictable. For code, keep low.
HallucinationWhen magic box LIE with confidence. Say function exist that NOT EXIST. Make up cave that not real. Normal. Always check.
RAGMagic box go READ REAL SCROLLS before answering, instead of just guessing from old memories. Much better.
Context engineeringArt of choosing WHAT to put in magic box head so it give good answer. The real skill.
MemoryWhat magic box remember between sleeps. Your preferences. Your cave layout. What made you angry last time.

Exercise

  1. Pick word that sound useful. Grunt at tool: “Why would I use [word]? Give simple example.”
  2. Try: “Explain [word] like me caveman. No big words.”

The short list

You don’t need to know everything about how LLMs work. But these terms come up constantly when configuring and using AI coding tools. Knowing them saves you from guessing at settings and helps you understand why your tool behaves the way it does.

Token - The unit AI models think in. Not exactly a word. Roughly 3/4 of a word on average. “function” is one token. “backgroundColor” might be three. Everything you send and receive costs tokens, and your tool has a budget per session.

Context window - The total number of tokens a model can hold at once. Think of it as working memory. A 200k context window sounds huge, but project instructions, file contents, and conversation history fill it fast. When it’s full, older messages get dropped.

Prompt - Everything you send to the model. Not just what you type. Your tool bundles your message with system instructions, file contents, and other context into one prompt behind the scenes.

System prompt - Instructions your tool gives the model before you say anything. This is where project instructions (like CLAUDE.md) end up. You usually don’t see it, but it shapes every response.

Temperature - Controls randomness. Low temperature (0) means the model picks the most likely next word every time. Higher temperature means more creative, less predictable output. Most coding tools keep this low for you.

Model - The specific AI you’re talking to. Claude Opus, Sonnet, GPT-4, Gemini. Different models have different strengths, speeds, and costs. Your tool usually lets you switch between them.

Agent - An AI that can take actions, not just answer questions. When your coding tool reads files, runs tests, and edits code on its own, it’s acting as an agent. Most modern coding tools are agents.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) - A standard way to connect AI tools to external services. Like a plugin system. An MCP server might give your AI access to your database, Slack, or GitHub. Check what’s connected, it matters.

Hallucination - When the model confidently states something that isn’t true. It might reference a function that doesn’t exist or invent an API. This is why verification matters.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) - A technique where the AI searches a knowledge base before answering, so it’s working from real data instead of just its training. Some tools use this to search your codebase.

Exercise

  1. Open your AI tool’s settings and find where it shows the model name and context window size.
  2. Ask your tool: “How many tokens have we used in this conversation so far?”

Terms that affect your daily workflow

These are grouped by when you’ll actually encounter them.

When you start a session

Context window - Your AI’s working memory. Measured in tokens. Everything in a session has to fit inside it: your messages, the AI’s responses, project instructions, file contents. When it fills up, the AI starts forgetting earlier parts of the conversation.

Token - The smallest piece of text the AI processes. Roughly 3/4 of a word. “Hello” is one token. A 500-line file might be 2,000 tokens. You’re always spending tokens, even before you type anything.

System prompt - Hidden instructions sent to the model before your first message. Your project instructions file (CLAUDE.md, copilot-instructions.md) becomes part of this. It’s why the AI already knows about your project.

When you’re working

Agent - An AI that does things, not just suggests things. Reads your files, runs commands, edits code, creates commits. If your tool is making changes to your codebase, it’s an agent.

Model - The specific AI brain behind your tool. Claude Opus is different from Sonnet is different from GPT-4. Each has different speed, cost, and capability tradeoffs. You can usually switch between them.

Temperature - How much the AI improvises. Low = predictable, deterministic. High = creative, varied. Coding tools default to low because you want consistent, correct code.

Hallucination - When the AI makes something up with total confidence. A function that doesn’t exist. A flag that was never added. This is normal, not a bug. Verify what it tells you.

When you’re configuring tools

MCP (Model Context Protocol) - A plug-in standard that connects your AI tool to external services like GitHub, databases, or Slack. Think of it like USB for AI tools. Check what’s connected.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) - When the AI searches real documents before answering, instead of relying on memory alone. Some tools use this to search your codebase or docs.

Prompt - Everything that gets sent to the model. Not just your message, but all the context your tool bundles with it. Understanding this helps you understand why responses change depending on what’s loaded.

Exercise

  1. Ask your tool: “How many tokens are in this conversation right now?” See if it can tell you.
  2. Ask: “What model are you? What’s your context window size?”
  3. Try asking the same question twice. Notice how the answer varies slightly. That’s temperature at work.

The terms that actually matter

You’ll see a lot of AI jargon. Most of it doesn’t matter for day-to-day coding. Here’s what does, explained through things you can see in your tool right now.

Tokens and context

Everything your AI tool processes is measured in tokens. One token is roughly 3/4 of a word. A typical source file is 500-2,000 tokens.

Your tool has a context window, a hard limit on how many tokens it can hold at once. This includes everything: your messages, the AI’s responses, files it reads, project instructions, and memory. When the window fills up, your tool starts compressing or dropping older content.

In Claude Code, type /context to see exactly how your tokens are being spent.

This is why long sessions get worse over time. The AI isn’t getting tired, it’s losing context.

Models

A model is the specific AI behind your tool. You can usually switch between them:

  • Faster, cheaper models (Sonnet, Haiku, GPT-4o-mini) for simple tasks
  • Slower, smarter models (Opus, GPT-4, Gemini Ultra) for complex reasoning

The model you pick changes speed, cost, and quality. Experiment.

Agents and tools

An agent is an AI that takes actions. If your tool reads files, runs tests, and edits code without you doing it manually, that’s an agent. Most modern AI coding tools are agents.

Agents connect to external services through MCP (Model Context Protocol), a standard plug-in system. An MCP server might give your AI access to GitHub, your database, or Slack. You should know what’s connected.

Things that go wrong

Hallucination is when the model states something false with confidence. It might reference a function that doesn’t exist or use an API incorrectly. This is normal. Always verify.

Temperature controls randomness. Your coding tool keeps it low so code is predictable. But it’s never zero for most tools, which is why asking the same question twice gives slightly different answers.

Exercise

  1. Find your tool’s context window size. Ask it: “What’s your context window, and how much have we used?”
  2. If your tool lets you switch models, try the same prompt with a fast model and a smart model. Notice the difference.

Core concepts

TermWhat it means
TokenThe unit AI reads and writes in. Roughly 3/4 of a word. Everything costs tokens.
Context windowHow much the AI can hold in memory at once. When it fills up, older stuff gets dropped.
PromptEverything sent to the model, not just what you typed. Includes system instructions, files, and history.
System promptHidden instructions your tool sends before you say anything. Your CLAUDE.md lives here.
ModelThe specific AI brain (Opus, Sonnet, GPT-4). Different speed, cost, and smarts. You can usually switch.

How your tool works

TermWhat it means
AgentAn AI that takes actions: reads files, runs tests, edits code. Most coding tools are agents now.
MCPModel Context Protocol. A plug-in system that connects your AI to external services like GitHub or Slack.
MemoryWhat your tool remembers across sessions. Preferences, facts about you, project context.
TemperatureControls randomness. Low = predictable code. High = creative writing. Coding tools keep it low.

Things to watch for

TermWhat it means
HallucinationWhen the AI confidently makes something up. A fake function, a wrong API. Normal, not a bug. Verify.
RAGRetrieval-Augmented Generation. The AI searches real docs before answering instead of guessing from memory.
Context engineeringThe practice of controlling what information the AI sees so it gives better answers.

Exercise

  1. Ask your tool: “What model are you, and what’s your context window size?”
  2. Ask: “How many tokens have we used so far?”

What you’re paying for

TermThe gist
TokenThe smallest unit of text the AI processes. ~3/4 of a word. A 200-line file is about 800 tokens.
Context windowThe AI’s total working memory for one session. 200k tokens sounds big, but fills fast with files and history.
ModelWhich AI you’re talking to. Opus/Sonnet/Haiku, GPT-4/4o-mini. Bigger = smarter and slower.

What’s happening behind the scenes

TermThe gist
PromptThe full package sent to the model: your message + system instructions + file contents + conversation history.
System promptInstructions injected before your first message. Your project config file (CLAUDE.md, copilot-instructions.md) ends up here.
AgentAI that acts, not just answers. Reads files, runs commands, writes code. If it’s editing your repo, it’s an agent.
MCPModel Context Protocol. How your AI connects to external tools like GitHub, databases, Slack. Think USB-C for AI.
TemperatureDial from predictable (0) to creative (1). Coding tools stay low. That’s why code suggestions are consistent.

What can go wrong

TermThe gist
HallucinationThe AI invents something that doesn’t exist and says it with total confidence. Check its work.
Context overflowWhen your session exceeds the context window. The AI starts dropping older messages. Sessions degrade over time.

Concepts you’ll hear about

TermThe gist
RAGRetrieval-Augmented Generation. AI searches real documents before answering instead of relying on training data alone.
Context engineeringDeliberately shaping what information the AI sees to get better results. The skill behind good AI tool use.
MemoryPersistent info your tool carries across sessions. Preferences, project facts, past corrections.

Exercise

  1. Ask your tool: “What model are you using right now? What’s the context window?”
  2. Ask: “Without doing any research, what do these terms mean: token, context window, hallucination?”
TermWhat you need to know
TokenHow AI measures text. ~3/4 of a word. You’re spending tokens every time you send a message or the AI reads a file.
Context windowThe AI’s memory limit for one session. Everything has to fit: your messages, its responses, files, instructions. When full, old stuff gets dropped.
ModelThe AI behind your tool (Opus, Sonnet, GPT-4). You can usually switch. Faster models are cheaper but less capable.
PromptEverything sent to the model. Not just your message, but system instructions, files, and history bundled together.
System promptHidden instructions your tool sends before you type anything. Your CLAUDE.md or copilot-instructions.md lives here.
AgentAI that takes actions: reads files, runs tests, edits code, creates commits. Most AI coding tools are agents.
MCPModel Context Protocol. Plug-in system connecting your AI to external services (GitHub, Slack, databases). Like USB-C for AI tools.
MemoryWhat your tool remembers between sessions. Your preferences, project context, past corrections.
TemperatureRandomness dial. Low = deterministic, predictable code. High = creative, varied output. Coding tools keep it low.
HallucinationAI confidently stating something false. A function that doesn’t exist, a wrong API signature. Normal. Always verify.
RAGRetrieval-Augmented Generation. AI searches real documents before answering instead of guessing from training data.
Context engineeringThe practice of controlling what the AI sees so it gives better results. The real skill in using AI tools well.

Exercise

  1. Ask your tool: “What model are you? What’s your context window? How much have we used?”
  2. Ask: “Explain these to me like I’m a developer, not a data scientist: token, prompt, agent, hallucination.”
TermWhat you need to know
TokenThe unit AI reads and writes in. ~3/4 of a word. Everything costs tokens. A 200-line file is about 800.
Context windowThe AI’s memory limit for one session. Everything has to fit: your messages, its responses, files, instructions. When full, old stuff gets dropped.
ModelThe specific AI brain (Opus, Sonnet, GPT-4). Different speed, cost, and smarts. You can usually switch.
PromptEverything sent to the model. Not just your message, but system instructions, files, and history bundled together.
System promptHidden instructions your tool sends before you type anything. Your CLAUDE.md or copilot-instructions.md lives here.
AgentAI that takes actions: reads files, runs tests, edits code, creates commits. Most AI coding tools are agents.
SubagentA child agent spawned by the main agent to handle a specific subtask. Like delegating a code review or search to a specialist while the main agent continues working.
MCPModel Context Protocol. Plug-in system connecting your AI to external services (GitHub, Slack, databases). Like USB-C for AI tools.
SkillsPortable packages of instructions and scripts that extend what your AI agent can do. Reusable recipes for specific workflows like PR reviews or deployments.
HooksAutomated actions that fire at specific points in the agent’s workflow. Unlike skills, hooks run outside the AI’s reasoning. They enforce rules the AI can’t skip.
CommandsSlash commands (like /context, /init, /memory) built into your tool. Shortcuts for common actions that don’t require natural language.
Rules / InstructionsConfiguration files that tell the AI how to behave in your project. CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules/, copilot-instructions.md. Loaded every session automatically.
PluginsExtensions that add capabilities to your tool. MCP servers are the modern version. Older tools used proprietary plugin formats.
Human-in-the-loopA workflow where the AI proposes changes but a human approves before they’re applied. The default for most coding agents. The alternative is full autonomy.
Dark codeCode generated by AI that no human has read or understood. Builds up when you accept suggestions without reviewing. Creates invisible tech debt.
Agent orchestrationRunning multiple agents in parallel on different tasks. The Codex desktop app and Cursor 3 are built around this concept.
TemperatureRandomness dial. Low = deterministic, predictable code. High = creative, varied output. Coding tools keep it low.
HallucinationAI confidently stating something false. A function that doesn’t exist, a wrong API signature. Normal. Always verify.
RAGRetrieval-Augmented Generation. AI searches real documents before answering instead of guessing from training data.
Context engineeringDeliberately shaping what information the AI sees so it gives better results. The real skill in using AI tools well.
MemoryWhat your tool remembers between sessions. Your preferences, project context, past corrections.

Exercise

  1. Ask your tool: “What model are you? What’s your context window? How much have we used?”
  2. Ask: “Explain these to me like I’m a developer, not a data scientist: token, prompt, agent, hallucination.”

Your agent runs where you left it

You started a refactor in Claude Code before lunch. Now you’re on your phone at a coffee shop and want to check in. Or you’re on the couch and want to kick off a test suite so it’s done by morning.

This isn’t a future workflow. The tools for it exist right now.

Three ways to work remotely

1. Remote control your session

Start a session on your machine, then connect to it from another device.

In Claude Code, type /rc in a running session. Scan the QR code with your phone and you’re connected to the live session from a browser. You can send messages, approve actions, and steer the agent.

In Copilot CLI, start with copilot --remote or type /remote mid-session. It generates a link or QR code that opens a live view on GitHub’s web or mobile app. You can switch between plan, interactive, and autopilot modes from your phone.

2. Message it from an app you already use

Claude Code Channels (launched March 2026) lets you connect a running session to Telegram, Discord, or iMessage. Send a message to your bot, and it runs with full filesystem and git access, then replies in the same thread.

OpenClaw takes this further. It’s an open-source agent (247K GitHub stars) that connects to 23+ messaging platforms: WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack, Signal, iMessage, Teams, and more. One instance on a server, accessible from every app you already have.

3. Dispatch and walk away

Sometimes you don’t need to steer. You just need to say “do this” and check back later.

Claude Dispatch (Pro/Max) creates a persistent thread between the Claude app on your phone and Claude Desktop on your computer. Send a task from your phone, your computer executes it in the background.

GitHub Copilot coding agent works from GitHub Mobile. Assign an issue to Copilot, close the app, come back to a PR. You can also kick off tasks from the web and steer them from your phone.

The Codex App runs agents in cloud sandboxes. No local machine needed. Dispatch work from the desktop app or chatgpt.com/codex and check back when it’s done.

The DIY approach: SSH + tmux

If you want full control without vendor features, the classic setup still works:

  1. Run your agent in a tmux session on your dev machine (or a VPS)
  2. Connect from your phone with an SSH app like Termius or Blink Shell
  3. Use mosh instead of SSH if you’re on flaky mobile connections (it handles WiFi-to-cellular transitions)
  4. Add ntfy (an MCP server) so your agent sends push notifications to your phone when it finishes a task

This gives you the same “agent running persistently, accessible from anywhere” setup, just without a polished mobile UI.

Exercise

  1. Start a coding agent session on your machine. Then try connecting to it from your phone using one of these methods:
    • /rc in Claude Code, or /remote in Copilot CLI
    • SSH + tmux from a mobile terminal app
  2. From your phone, give the agent a small task (rename a variable, add a test) and watch it work.

The tether is optional

Most people think of coding agents as something you sit in front of. Terminal open, watching tokens stream. But the agent doesn’t need your screen. It needs your codebase, your tools, and occasional direction. Those can come from anywhere.

How connected do you need to be?

It depends on the task.

Quick check-in — “Is it done? Did the tests pass?”

Both Claude Code (/rc) and Copilot CLI (/remote) let you connect to a live session from your phone via QR code. You get a live view of the session in a browser. Approve an action, send a steering message, then put your phone away.

Async dispatch — “Do this while I’m gone.”

Assign a GitHub issue to Copilot from GitHub Mobile. Or send a task to Claude Dispatch from the Claude app on your phone. Or queue something in the Codex App’s cloud sandbox. In all three cases, you close the app and come back to a result (a PR, a diff, a completed task).

Ongoing conversation — “I want to message my agent like a coworker.”

Claude Code Channels connects a running session to Telegram, Discord, or iMessage. You message it, it runs commands with full access to your repo, and replies in the same thread. OpenClaw does the same thing across 23+ messaging platforms, open source, running on your own hardware.

Full terminal access — “I want the real thing on my phone.”

SSH into your machine from Termius or Blink Shell, reattach to a tmux session, and you’re in the exact same terminal. Use mosh instead of SSH if you’re switching between WiFi and cellular. This is the most powerful option and the least polished.

The persistent session pattern

All of these share one idea: the agent session outlives your connection to it.

The key ingredients:

  • tmux or screen keeps the process alive when you disconnect
  • Tailscale or Cloudflare Tunnel gives you a secure path back to your machine from anywhere
  • ntfy (via MCP) pushes notifications to your phone when the agent finishes or needs input
  • mosh handles flaky mobile connections gracefully

Whether you use vendor remote-control features or DIY with SSH, the pattern is the same: start the work, detach, reconnect when you need to.

Exercise

  1. Start Claude Code or Copilot CLI in a tmux session on your machine.
  2. From your phone, connect to it (try /rc, /remote, or plain SSH).
  3. Give the agent a task and disconnect. Reconnect 5 minutes later and check the result.

You don’t have to be there

A coding agent is a process running on a machine. It doesn’t care whether you’re watching. The question is just: how do you talk to it when you’re somewhere else?

There are four approaches, and they’re not mutually exclusive.

Remote control

Connect to a live session from another device. You see what the agent sees.

ToolCommandWhat happens
Claude Code/rcQR code opens a live session in your phone’s browser
Copilot CLIcopilot --remote or /remoteLink/QR opens session on GitHub web or mobile app

Both let you send messages, approve actions, and steer the agent. The session runs on your machine; your phone is just a window into it.

Messaging channels

Talk to your agent from apps you already use. The agent runs on your machine (or a server); messages arrive through a chat platform.

Claude Code Channels (March 2026) connects a session to Telegram, Discord, or iMessage. The agent has full filesystem and git access. You message it like a coworker, it replies in the same thread.

OpenClaw is open source (247K stars) and connects to 23+ platforms: WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack, Signal, iMessage, Teams, IRC, and more. You run one instance and reach it from any app. It’s not coding-specific, but developers use it to dispatch tasks, check build status, and get notifications.

Dispatch

Fire and forget. Assign work, walk away, come back to a result.

  • GitHub Mobile: Assign an issue to Copilot. It spins up a GitHub Actions environment, writes code, runs tests, opens a PR.
  • Claude Dispatch: Send a task from the Claude phone app to Claude Desktop on your computer. It executes in the background.
  • Codex App: Cloud sandboxes. No local machine needed. Dispatch from desktop or web.

SSH + tmux (the universal method)

Every approach above is a vendor feature that might change. The one that always works:

  1. Run your agent in tmux on your dev machine or a VPS
  2. SSH in from your phone with Termius (iOS/Android) or Blink Shell (iOS)
  3. Reattach to the session: tmux attach

Upgrades that make it better:

  • mosh instead of SSH: survives WiFi/cellular switches and sleep
  • Tailscale: encrypted tunnel to your machine, no port forwarding
  • ntfy MCP server: agent sends push notifications to your phone when it needs you or finishes a task

Exercise

  1. Pick the method that fits your setup:
    • If you use Claude Code: try /rc and scan from your phone
    • If you use Copilot CLI: try /remote
    • If you want the DIY route: install tmux, start a session, SSH from a phone app
  2. From your phone, ask the agent: “What are you working on?” or give it a small task.
  3. Disconnect. Come back in 5 minutes and see what happened.

You have a commit style, PR sections, ticket labels, draft-first defaults. Re-teaching the AI every session is wasted effort.

Skill teaches the AI how. Hook makes sure it actually does.

  • Skill = your format, saved once. The AI loads it on its own when the topic comes up. The recipe.
  • Hook = makes sure the AI actually uses it. The doorman.

Example prompts

Commits

I want to make a /commit skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings. Interview me.
I want a hook in my user settings that makes sure you always use my /commit skill before any git commit.

Pull requests

I want to make a /pr skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings, that always opens PRs as drafts and follows whatever PR template the current repo uses. Interview me.
I want a hook in my user settings that makes sure you use my /pr skill instead of running gh pr create on your own.

Issues

I want to make an /issue skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings, that creates tickets on my team's board with the right labels, fields, project, and current sprint. Interview me.

No hook for issues - they’re usually opt-in, not commands the AI runs unprompted.

Exercise

  1. Try to make a hook or skill for something you do often.
  2. Consider what else would be helpful as a skill or hook.

Use AI for compression, not delegation

The best use of AI in your Git workflow is to compress work you already understand:

  • Turn scattered notes into a clean issue.
  • Turn staged changes into a readable commit message.
  • Turn a diff into a PR summary reviewers can scan fast.

The risky use is asking AI to invent intent after the fact. If the model cannot see your real goal, it fills the gap with plausible filler. Keep it close to the source material and make one human pass before you publish.

Three good options

Option 1: GitHub-native

This is the lowest-friction path if your team already lives in GitHub.

  • Draft the issue with AI from a bug report, Slack thread, or TODO list.
  • Use Copilot commit message generation when committing in GitHub’s web UI.
  • Use Copilot PR summary generation from a blank PR description, then edit the result for reviewer context.

Research note: GitHub’s docs say PR summaries work best from a blank description, and they explicitly recommend manual review because summaries can be inaccurate.

Option 2: Editor-first

This is usually the fastest if you do most of your work locally.

  • Ask your editor chat to draft an issue from repo context: “Turn this bug into a GitHub issue with problem, repro, expected behavior, and acceptance criteria.”
  • Stage your files, then let Cursor generate the commit message from staged changes.
  • Ask chat for a PR body before you open GitHub: “Summarize these changes for reviewers. Include what changed, why, risks, and test notes.”

Research note: Cursor’s docs say commit generation uses staged changes plus repo history, which makes it better than asking for a commit message in a blank chat.

Option 3: Agent handoff

This is the highest automation option.

  • Write a clear GitHub issue.
  • Assign the issue to Copilot or start an agent task from chat.
  • Let the agent open the PR, then review the branch, commits, and PR text yourself.

This works best when the issue is already specific. GitHub’s docs note that after Copilot is assigned, later issue comments are not automatically part of its understanding. If requirements move, put the update on the PR.

Tool examples

In VS Code Copilot, try:

Draft a GitHub issue for this work.
Use sections for problem, scope, acceptance criteria, and out of scope.
Be concrete and keep it under 200 words.

Then after staging changes:

Write a PR description for reviewers.
Include what changed, why it changed, risks, and how to verify it.
Do not invent tests I did not run.

In Cursor, stage files first, then use the sparkle icon in the Git panel to generate the commit message. If your team uses Conventional Commits, check that the suggested prefix matches what actually changed.

Exercise

  1. Take one real change you made recently and draft all three artifacts with AI: issue, commit, and PR.
  2. For each artifact, ask the AI to use a fixed structure instead of “write me a good X.”
  3. Compare the draft against the actual diff and remove anything vague, inflated, or invented.

Pick your automation level

There are three sane ways to use AI for issues, commits, and PRs. The trick is matching the tool to the amount of judgment the step needs.

Option 1: Assistive

Use AI as a drafting layer only.

  • Issue: paste notes, ask for a crisp problem statement and acceptance criteria.
  • Commit: stage files, let the tool suggest a message, then trim it.
  • PR: ask for a reviewer-facing summary from the diff.

Best when the work is small and you already know what you changed.

Option 2: Structured

Use templates plus AI.

  • Issue: feed the model your team’s issue template and ask it to fill only the fields it can justify.
  • Commit: ask for one line that follows your team’s commit style.
  • PR: ask for exactly four sections, like summary, risk, test notes, rollout.

This usually gives the best results because the model has less room to ramble.

Option 3: Agentic

Use AI to carry the task across the line.

  • Start with a strong issue.
  • Let the agent implement and open the PR.
  • Review like you would review a junior engineer’s branch: check scope, check tests, check commit quality, check the PR body.

Best when the issue is specific and the branch can stand on its own.

Tool examples

In VS Code Copilot, a good issue prompt is:

Draft a GitHub issue from this context.
Use: problem, impact, acceptance criteria.
If something is uncertain, mark it as an open question instead of guessing.

For a PR:

Write a pull request description from my current diff.
Use these headings: Summary, Why, Risk, Validation.
Keep it short and factual.

In Cursor, generate the commit message from staged changes. Cursor’s docs say it adapts to your existing commit style, which is useful if your repo already has a pattern.

A good manual check is: does the commit message describe the staged diff, not the entire branch?

Exercise

  1. Pick one open task and draft the issue, commit message, and PR in assistive mode.
  2. Redo the same three artifacts in structured mode with headings or templates.
  3. Compare the outputs and decide where structure helped and where it just added ceremony.

Match the prompt to the evidence

Issues, commits, and PRs look similar because they are all text. But they need different evidence:

  • An issue should come from problem context.
  • A commit should come from the staged diff.
  • A PR should come from the whole branch diff plus reviewer intent.

When people get weak results, they often ask one chat window to do all three without changing the context.

Three options

Option 1: Notes to issue

Feed AI bug notes, a support ticket, or a design comment and ask it to turn that into a real issue. Good output has scope and acceptance criteria. Bad output sounds polished but says nothing testable.

Option 2: Staged diff to commit

Generate the commit message only after staging. This is where Cursor is strong: its commit generation is based on staged changes, not a vague memory of the session.

Option 3: Branch diff to PR

Generate the PR description from the actual branch diff. GitHub’s PR summary tools can save time, but GitHub also warns that the output may be inaccurate and needs review. Treat the generated draft as scaffolding, not truth.

Tool examples

In VS Code Copilot, try this sequence:

Turn these notes into a GitHub issue.
Keep only facts supported by the notes.
Add acceptance criteria as a checklist.

Then later:

Write a PR description from the current branch diff.
Explain reviewer focus areas.
Do not claim tests passed unless I explicitly told you they passed.

In Cursor, use AI commit generation after you stage one logical chunk. If the generated subject line tries to summarize unrelated changes, your staging is probably too broad.

Exercise

  1. Choose one recent branch and separate your source material into notes, staged diff, and full diff.
  2. Use AI once for each artifact with only the matching source material.
  3. Ask yourself which artifact improved most when the context was tighter.

Codify your format once

Every time you ask the AI to “write a good commit message,” you’re re-teaching it your team’s style. Skills are the fix: write it once, and the AI loads it whenever the topic comes up.

ArtifactSkill knowsHook can enforce
CommitYour subject line style, conventional commits prefix, body formatBlock direct git commit, force the skill
PRRequired sections, draft-first, who to tag, label rulesBlock git push without skill, draft default
IssueRequired fields (problem, repro, acceptance), board labels, assignee defaults(No hook - issues live outside git)

Example prompts

Commit skill

Interview me about how I want commit messages, then save it as a /commit skill.
Write a hook that runs my /commit skill before any git commit.

PR skill

Interview me about my PR format and save it as a /pr skill. Always draft-first.
Add a hook so the AI uses my /pr skill instead of running gh pr create directly.

Issue skill

Interview me about how I want issues created on my board, including required fields and labels. Save as a /issue skill.

Exercise

  1. Pick the artifact you reformat the most by hand (probably commits).
  2. Run the matching interview prompt and let the AI write the skill for you.

Let the AI interview you

You probably can’t recite your own commit format on the spot. But you’ll recognize it when you see it. So instead of writing a skill from scratch, have the AI walk you through it.

The same pattern works for all three artifacts: commits, PRs, issues. Two prompts each. First one builds the skill. Second one wires it in.

Example prompts

Commits

I want a /commit skill that knows my format. Interview me with one question at a time. When done, save the skill.
Show me a hook that nudges the AI to use my /commit skill before any git commit.

Pull requests

I want a /pr skill that always opens PRs as drafts and uses my team's section structure. Interview me one question at a time, then save it.
Show me a hook so the AI uses /pr instead of running gh pr create on its own.

Issues

I want an /issue skill that creates tickets on my board with the right labels and required fields. Interview me one question at a time, then save the skill.

What good interview questions look like

A good interview prompt doesn’t dump 12 questions on you. It asks:

  1. Where do these get stored? (~/.claude/skills/?)
  2. What’s a recent example I could pattern off?
  3. One specific question, wait for answer, next question.

If the AI starts writing the skill before asking enough questions, tell it to slow down.

Exercise

  1. Pick one of the three interview prompts above.
  2. Answer the questions one at a time.
  3. Check the resulting skill file. Is it short enough that you’d actually maintain it?

Pair each skill with a hook

A skill alone is a suggestion. The AI might use it, might forget, might wing it. A hook is the enforcement layer: when the AI tries to commit or open a PR without going through the skill, the hook stops it.

Think of it as: skill = the recipe, hook = the doorman.

Example prompts

Commits: skill + hook

Interview me, then save a /commit skill that knows my message format.
Write a PreToolUse hook that catches git commit and tells the AI to use /commit first.

PRs: skill + hook (draft-first)

Interview me, then save a /pr skill that always opens PRs as drafts.
Write a hook that catches gh pr create and tells the AI to use /pr instead.

Issues: skill only

Interview me, then save an /issue skill that knows my board's required fields and labels. (No hook - issues are usually opt-in, not commands the AI runs unprompted.)

What “interview me” should look like

A good interview prompt walks one question at a time. If the AI dumps 10 questions in a single message, push back: “ask one at a time and wait for my answer.”

If the AI starts writing the skill before it has enough info, ask “what else do you need to know?” before letting it save.

Exercise

  1. Build the /commit skill first. It’s the highest-frequency one.
  2. Once it works, add the matching hook so future sessions can’t skip it.

Codify your opinions, then enforce them

You have a commit style, PR sections, ticket labels, draft-first defaults. Re-teaching the AI every session is wasted effort.

  • Skill = your format, saved once. The AI loads it on its own when the topic comes up. The recipe.
  • Hook = makes sure the AI actually uses it. The doorman.

Example prompts

Commits

I want to make a /commit skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings. Interview me.
I want a hook in my user settings that makes sure you always use my /commit skill before any git commit.

Pull requests

I want to make a /pr skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings, that always opens PRs as drafts and follows whatever PR template the current repo uses. Interview me.
I want a hook in my user settings that makes sure you use my /pr skill instead of running gh pr create on your own.

Issues

I want to make an /issue skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings, that creates tickets on my team's board with the right labels, fields, project, and current sprint. Interview me.

No hook for issues - they’re usually opt-in, not commands the AI runs unprompted.

What “interview me” should look like

One question at a time, waiting for your answer. If the AI dumps 10 questions in a single message, push back: “ask one at a time and wait for my answer.” If it starts writing the skill before it has enough info, ask “what else do you need to know?” before letting it save.

Exercise

  1. Build the /commit skill first. It’s the highest-frequency one.
  2. Once it works, add the matching hook so future sessions can’t skip it.

Pick a guardrail

GuardrailWhat it isBypass difficulty
PermissionsRules the AI reads (allow / ask / deny)Easy (encoding, alt commands)
HooksTriggers that fire on tool calls (pause, block, log, or run a script)Medium (regex evasion, async bugs)
SandboxOS blocks writes outside the project and limits networkHard (kernel-enforced)
ContainerThe agent runs inside Docker, isolated from your real machine and filesVery hard

Heads up
If you run the prompts below, the changes are permanent until you go back and undo them. Next session you’ll still be locked down. Audit first so you know what you’re changing.

Example prompts

Example: audit what you already have (run this first)

Summarize my current guardrails in plain language: permissions, hooks,
sandbox, container. Just the highlights, not every rule. Call out
anything that looks risky.

Permissions

In my user settings, deny reads of every common credential location for a typical dev machine. List what you added.
In my user settings, ask before any rm -rf (except node_modules) or sudo.
In this project's settings, auto-allow my test and lint commands.

Hooks

Write a user-level hook that blocks rm -rf (except node_modules) and sudo.
Write a hook that runs lint before any git commit, and blocks the commit if lint fails.
Suggest a useful PostToolUse hook for this project.

Sandbox

Is my agent's sandbox actually on right now? Some are opt-in.
Turn on the strictest OS sandbox my agent supports (Landlock, Seatbelt, or Bubblewrap).
What's the difference between my agent's sandbox modes?

Container

Set up a devcontainer so I can safely run my agent with --dangerously-skip-permissions.
Add a firewall allowlist to my devcontainer for only the hosts this project needs.
Why use a container if I already have a sandbox? Two sentences.

Two tools that show this clearly

In Claude Code, the audit prompt above will name your .claude/settings.json rules, your sandbox profile, and whether you’re inside a devcontainer.

In Codex CLI, the startup banner already shows your sandbox mode. The audit prompt will explain what it allows and what triggers an approval prompt.

Exercise

  1. Run the audit prompt to see what you already have.
  2. Pick any one of the example prompts above and try it.

Permission tiers, not on-or-off

Every agent has a knob between “ask before each action” and “do whatever you want.” Most people set it once and forget it. The trick is to slide that knob based on the work, not your mood.

A useful way to think about it:

  • Tier 0 - Approve every action. Slow. Use it on production code, infrastructure, secrets, anything you can’t afford to undo.
  • Tier 1 - Allowlist safe commands. Tests, linters, builds, formatters. Anything destructive still asks.
  • Tier 2 - Auto-mode with deny-rules. Agent decides. Specific dangerous commands are blocked. You glance at the diff.
  • Tier 3 - Full auto, inside a sandbox. The agent runs unattended. Safety comes from the OS or container, not the agent itself.

Tier 3 only works if the sandbox is real. Without one, “skip permissions” is just trust. With one, it’s freedom.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, permissions live in .claude/settings.json. Three rule types: allow, ask, deny. Deny always wins.

{
  "permissions": {
    "defaultMode": "acceptEdits",
    "allow": ["Bash(npm test:*)", "Bash(npm run lint:*)", "Read"],
    "ask": ["Edit", "Bash(git push:*)"],
    "deny": ["Bash(rm -rf:*)", "Read(./.env*)", "WebFetch"]
  }
}

You can also flip modes mid-session with /permissions or hit Shift+Tab to cycle between plan, default, and accept-edits. For unattended work, --dangerously-skip-permissions exists, but only run it inside a devcontainer or VM.

In Cursor, agent mode has an allowlist, a denylist, and a “prevent file deletion” toggle. YOLO/auto-run executes everything that isn’t denied. Heads up: security researchers found four ways to bypass Cursor’s denylist using shell tricks like && chaining and base64 encoding. Treat the denylist as a speed bump, not a wall.

Exercise

  1. Open your tool’s permission settings. Write down your current tier (0 to 3).
  2. Pick one task you’re about to do. Decide which tier fits, and either tighten or loosen the settings before you start.
  3. Try Tier 1 if you’ve never used it: add 3 to 5 specific safe commands to the allowlist (your test runner, your linter, your formatter) and see how many fewer prompts you hit.

One layer is not enough

There is a real story behind this kata. A Claude Code user asked it to clean up a project. The agent ran rm -rf ~/ and removed their home directory. Another agent, in a separate incident, found a way around its own denylist and tried to disable the sandbox. In 2025, a hidden prompt injection in a pull request description triggered remote code execution through GitHub Copilot.

The lesson is not “AI is unsafe.” The lesson is that any single guardrail will eventually fail. The fix is layers.

The four common layers

  1. In-agent permissions. Allow, ask, deny rules in the tool’s config. Lowest friction, weakest guarantee. The agent sees these rules, so a clever prompt or a malicious diff can sometimes talk around them.
  2. OS sandbox. The harness uses the kernel to restrict reads, writes, and network calls. The agent literally cannot write outside the project, even if it tries.
  3. Container or VM. Docker, devcontainer, or microVM. The blast radius is the container, not your machine. Your SSH keys, AWS credentials, and home dir are not mounted.
  4. Network allowlist. The container can only reach a list of domains. Even if the agent gets owned, it can’t exfiltrate to a random host.

Each layer assumes the layer above it might fail.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, permissions and sandboxing are two separate systems. Permissions filter tool calls in the agent. Sandboxing enforces filesystem and network rules at the OS level. The official Anthropic devcontainer adds container isolation and a firewall on top, which is why the docs only recommend --dangerously-skip-permissions inside that container.

// .claude/settings.json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": ["Read(./.env*)", "Bash(curl:*)"]
  }
}

That deny rule is layer 1. Inside a devcontainer with the bundled firewall, layer 3 and 4 also kick in, so even a bypassed deny rule has limited reach.

In Codex CLI, the sandbox and the approval policy are independent. Sandbox modes go read-only to workspace-write to danger-full-access. Approval policy decides when Codex must stop and ask before crossing a sandbox boundary. The sandbox itself is enforced by the OS.

# ~/.codex/config.toml
sandbox_mode = "workspace-write"
approval_policy = "on-request"

Workspace-write turns network off by default. That single line is more protective than any prompt rule.

Exercise

  1. Count your layers. Are you running with just in-agent permissions? Or do you have a sandbox, container, and network rules too?
  2. Add one layer you don’t have. The easiest wins are usually a deny rule for ~/.ssh and .env files, or moving to a devcontainer.
  3. Ask your agent: “what guardrails are currently in effect for this session?” Compare its answer to what’s actually in your config.

Inside vs outside the agent

Guardrails come in two flavors:

  • Inside the agent: prompts, system messages, allow/deny rules the model reads. These are advisory. A good model usually follows them. A confused model, a clever prompt, or a malicious diff can route around them.
  • Outside the agent: OS permissions, sandbox profiles, container boundaries, network firewalls. These are physical. The agent cannot violate them because the kernel will not let it.

If your only guardrail is “I told it not to,” you have advice, not a guardrail.

Why this matters

Cursor’s YOLO denylist was bypassed four different ways by encoding commands in base64 or chaining with &&. The denylist was a string the agent could see and rewrite around. An OS-level rule like “this process cannot write outside /workspace” cannot be base64’d away.

In agent research papers, the same point keeps showing up: 94% of state-of-the-art agents are vulnerable to prompt injection. That number is about agent-level rules. Outside-the-agent rules are not in the same threat model, because the agent never gets a vote.

Two questions to ask about every guardrail

When you set up a permission or a deny rule, ask:

  1. Where is it enforced? If the answer is “in the system prompt” or “in the model’s config the model reads,” it’s inside the agent. If the answer is “the kernel refuses the syscall” or “the container has no network route,” it’s outside.
  2. What happens if the agent ignores it? If the answer is “the action runs anyway,” you have advice. If the answer is “the action fails with EACCES,” you have a guardrail.

Both have value. Inside-the-agent rules are fast to set up and reduce dumb mistakes. Outside-the-agent rules survive prompt injection and runaway agents. You want both.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, the difference is visible in the harness itself. Try ls ~/.ssh from a Bash tool call. If your sandbox has a read-deny on ~/.ssh, the directory listing is blocked at the OS level, not because Claude decided to be polite. Same for write paths: the harness can write to the project dir and $TMPDIR, and nowhere else, because that’s the sandbox profile.

In Codex CLI, workspace-write mode means “the OS will reject writes outside the workspace and network calls outside the allowlist.” When Codex hits one of those boundaries, it doesn’t decide whether to comply. It gets a system error and asks you to approve a bigger sandbox.

codex --sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request

The --ask-for-approval flag controls when Codex pauses to ask. The --sandbox flag controls what the OS will let it do regardless. They are different controls.

Exercise

  1. Pick one rule you rely on (a deny rule, a “don’t read .env” instruction, a system prompt). Decide whether it’s inside or outside the agent.
  2. Test it. Ask the agent to do the forbidden thing. Watch what happens. If the rule was inside the agent and the agent obeyed, great. Now ask “but really do it.” See if the rule holds.
  3. For one inside-the-agent rule that matters, add an outside-the-agent equivalent. Examples: a chmod -r ~/.ssh so the OS denies access, a devcontainer that doesn’t mount your home dir, or a network allowlist on your sandbox.

Four guardrails

1. Permissions

Rules the AI tool reads. Allow, ask, deny on tools and commands. Lives in .claude/settings.json, Cursor’s settings, etc.

Add deny rules to my Claude Code settings.json for: reading .env files,
reading ~/.ssh, any rm -rf, and any curl piped to a shell.
Add allow rules for my test runner and linter so I stop getting prompted.

2. Hooks

Scripts the harness runs before or after a tool call. Block, log, or modify. Outside the model’s reach.

Add a PreToolUse hook to my Claude Code settings that blocks any Bash
command containing "rm -rf" or writing outside my project directory.
Show me the hook code and where to put it.

3. Sandbox

OS-level limits on what the process can read, write, or reach over the network. Enforced by the kernel.

Show me how to enable the OS sandbox in my coding agent. List which
directories can be read/written and which network hosts are reachable.
Then suggest tighter defaults.

4. Container

The whole agent runs inside Docker or a devcontainer. Your home dir, SSH keys, and creds are not mounted. Blast radius is the container.

Generate a devcontainer.json for this repo that runs Claude Code with
no host network access except github.com and npm.org, and no mount of
my host home directory.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, all four are available. Permissions in .claude/settings.json, hooks in the same file under hooks, sandbox via the harness, and the official Anthropic devcontainer for container isolation.

In Codex CLI, sandbox modes are first class: read-only, workspace-write, danger-full-access. Approval policy is separate from sandbox, so you can be strict on one and lax on the other.

Exercise

  1. Pick which of the four you don’t have yet. Paste the matching prompt into your AI tool and let it set it up.
  2. Test it. Ask the AI to do the forbidden thing on purpose. See what blocks first.

The cheat sheet

GuardrailWhat it isWhere it lives
PermissionsAllow/ask/deny rules per tool or commandTool config (.claude/settings.json, Cursor settings)
HooksScripts that fire before/after a tool callTool config, runs in the harness
SandboxOS-level read/write/network limitsKernel (seatbelt, landlock, namespaces)
ContainerThe whole agent runs in an isolated boxDocker, devcontainer, microVM
WorktreeEach agent gets its own checkoutgit worktree add
Network allowlistOnly specific domains reachableFirewall in the container or sandbox

Stack as many as you need. None of them replace the others.

Prompts to set them up

Paste these into your agent. Each one configures one layer.

Permissions:

Audit my current permission rules for this AI tool. Then suggest a tighter
default: deny .env reads, deny ~/.ssh reads, deny rm -rf, allow my test
and lint commands. Update the config file and show me the diff.

Hooks:

Write a PreToolUse hook for Claude Code that blocks any Bash command
matching a list of dangerous patterns I'll give you. Start with: rm -rf,
curl piped to shell, sudo, chmod 777. Put it in the right place.

Sandbox:

What OS-level sandbox does my coding agent use right now? Show me the
read deny list, write allow list, and network allow list. Suggest
tighter defaults for a typical web project.

Container:

Generate a devcontainer.json that runs my coding agent isolated from
my host. No mount of ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, or my home dir. Network limited
to github.com, npmjs.com, and localhost.

Worktree:

Set up a git worktree workflow so I can run multiple agents in parallel
without them stepping on each other. Show me the commands to add and
remove worktrees.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, the order of strength is: permissions (advisory) → hooks (blocking) → sandbox (kernel) → container (full isolation). The official Anthropic devcontainer combines the last three.

In Codex CLI, sandbox is the headline feature: --sandbox workspace-write is the safe default, --ask-for-approval on-request controls when it pauses.

Exercise

  1. Run the “Permissions” prompt above and let your agent update your config.
  2. Run the “Sandbox” prompt and read what your sandbox actually permits. Most people are surprised by what’s open.

Pick a guardrail

  • Permissions = rules the AI reads (allow/ask/deny).
  • Hooks = scripts that intercept tool calls.
  • Sandbox = OS blocks reads, writes, network.
  • Container = the agent runs in a box.

Prompts

Lock down permissions

Update my AI tool's settings to:
- Deny reads of .env, .env.*, and ~/.ssh
- Deny any rm -rf or sudo
- Ask before git push, npm publish, or any write outside this repo
- Auto-allow my test runner, linter, and formatter
Show me the final config and where it lives.

Add a hook that blocks dangerous commands

Write a pre-tool-use hook for my agent that blocks Bash commands matching
this list: rm -rf, sudo, chmod 777, curl | sh, anything writing to /etc.
Put it in the right config file and tell me how to test it.

Turn on the sandbox

Show me how to enable my coding agent's strictest sandbox mode that still
lets it edit files in this repo and run tests. Tell me what it blocks
and what to expect.

Run inside a container

Create a devcontainer setup so my AI agent runs isolated from my host.
Do not mount ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.gnupg, or my home dir. Limit network
to github.com, npmjs.com, and pypi.org. Provide the devcontainer.json,
Dockerfile, and a one-liner to start it.

Audit what you already have

Without changing anything, list every guardrail currently active in this
session: permissions, hooks, sandbox, container, network rules. For each,
say where it's enforced and what would happen if you tried to violate it.

Two tools that show this clearly

In Claude Code, the audit prompt above will name your .claude/settings.json rules, your sandbox profile, and whether you’re inside a devcontainer.

In Codex CLI, the startup banner already shows your sandbox mode. The audit prompt will explain what it allows and what triggers an approval prompt.

Exercise

  1. Run the audit prompt first. See what you have.
  2. Run one setup prompt for a layer you don’t have yet.
  3. Try to break it. Ask the AI to read a .env or run rm -rf. Watch what stops it.
AI Katas — Internal Use Only
